fl-q3.text: How much oil is on the ground?

fl-a1.actionText: Using a flathead screwdriver, tighten the hose clamp 1/4 turn. Check that the hose is fully seated on the fitting. Wipe the area dry and observe for 2 minutes.

fl-q4.text: Is the leak stopped after tightening?

fl-a2.actionText: Ensure the overflow reservoir cap is seated properly. Press down and turn clockwise until it clicks. Check the coolant level — if below MIN, top off with coolant from the vehicle BII.

fl-q5.text: Is the reservoir cap now sealed and coolant at proper level?

options.label: No — cap is damaged or coolant still leaking

fl-a3.actionText: Small seepage (dampness, no drips) is within normal limits. Note the location and monitor. Check oil level on dipstick — if above ADD mark, vehicle is operational.

fl-t1.text: Transmission fluid leak detected. This requires maintenance support — do not operate.

terminal.summary: Transmission fluid leak (red/pink fluid) — beyond 10-level

fl-t2.text: Brake fluid leak detected. This is a safety-critical NO-GO. Do not operate the vehicle.

terminal.summary: Brake fluid leak — NMC, safety-critical
Coolant leak — beyond operator repair

fl-t4.text: Steady oil drip found. Vehicle should not be operated until inspected by maintenance.

terminal.summary: Steady oil drip — needs maintenance

fl-t5.text: Major oil leak (large puddle or stream). Do not operate. Immediate maintenance required.

terminal.summary: Major oil leak (large puddle) — NMC, immediate maintenance

fl-t6.text: Leak repaired. Hose clamp tightened successfully.

terminal.summary: Coolant leak at radiator hose — tightened clamp, leak stopped

fl-t7.text: Overflow reservoir sealed and coolant topped off.

terminal.summary: Overflow reservoir cap reseated, coolant topped off

fl-t8.text: Minor oil seepage (dampness, no drips) noted. Vehicle is operational — monitor on next PMCS.

terminal.summary: Minor oil seepage — within limits, monitoring

td-q2.text: Check tread depth — is the wear indicator bar visible (flush with tread)?

td-a1.actionText: Inflate to the pressure listed on the tire placard (typically 50 PSI front, 35 PSI rear for M1151). Use the vehicle CTIS if available, or external air source.

td-q3.text: Does the tire hold pressure after inflation?

td-t1.text: Tire is flat and cannot be repaired at operator level.

terminal.summary: Flat tire — needs replacement

td-t2.text: Sidewall damage makes the tire unsafe. Do not operate.

terminal.summary: Tire sidewall cut/bulge — NMC, needs replacement

td-t3.text: Tread is worn below minimum. Tire needs replacement.

terminal.summary: Tire tread below minimum — needs replacement

td-t4.text: Tire tread is worn but still serviceable. Note and monitor.

terminal.summary: Tire tread wearing but above minimum — monitor
Low tire pressure — inflated, holding

ws-q1.text: What happens when you turn the ignition to START?

options.label: Nothing — engine does not crank at all
Cranks slowly but won't turn over
Cranks at normal speed but does not start

ws-q2.text: Is the transmission shift lever in PARK (P)?

options.label: No — it was in another position

ws-a1.actionText: Move the transmission shift lever fully into the PARK (P) position. The neutral safety switch prevents cranking in other positions. Try starting again.

ws-q3.text: Check the battery condition. Are the battery terminals clean, tight, and free of corrosion?

ws-a2.actionText: Disconnect negative cable first, then positive. Clean terminals and cable clamps with wire brush or scraper. Reconnect positive first, then negative. Ensure connections are tight. Try starting again.

ws-q5.text: Attempt a slave-start from another vehicle using the slave receptacle. Does the engine crank?

options.label: Yes — engine starts with slave cable

ws-q6.text: Engine cranks slowly. Check the battery — are terminals clean and tight?

ws-a3.actionText: Disconnect negative cable first, then positive. Clean terminals and cable clamps with wire brush or scraper. Reconnect positive first, then negative. Ensure connections are tight. Try starting again.

ws-q7a.text: Does the engine crank normally now?

ws-q7.text: Attempt a slave-start from another vehicle. Does the engine start?

options.label: Yes — engine starts with slave cable
No — still cranks slowly or won't start

ws-q8.text: Engine cranks normally but does not start. Check the fuel gauge — is there fuel in the tank?

options.label: Fuel gauge reads empty or near empty

ws-a4.actionText: Fill the fuel tank with DF-2 diesel (or JP-8 if authorized). After fueling, you may need to purge air from the fuel system. Crank the engine for 10-15 seconds, wait 30 seconds, then try again up to 3 times.

ws-q10.text: Does the engine start after adding fuel?

ws-q9.text: Check the WAIT-TO-START indicator lamp. Did you wait for it to go out before cranking?

options.label: Yes — waited for lamp to go out, still won't start

ws-a5.actionText: Turn ignition to RUN. Wait for the WAIT-TO-START lamp to go out (may take up to 30 seconds in cold weather). Only then turn to START. Crank for no more than 15 seconds. If engine does not start, wait 30 seconds before next attempt.

ws-q11.text: Does the engine start after following the WAIT-TO-START procedure?

ws-t1.text: Engine started successfully. Issue was operator procedure.

terminal.summary: Engine start issue resolved — operator procedure corrected

ws-t2.text: Engine started with slave cable. Dead battery suspected — notify maintenance for battery replacement.

terminal.summary: Engine started with slave-start — battery weak, needs maintenance evaluation

ws-t3.text: Engine fails to crank even with slave power. Starter or electrical fault beyond operator level. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine won't crank — starter/electrical fault, needs maintenance

ws-t4.text: Engine started after refueling. Ensure fuel level is monitored.

terminal.summary: Engine started after refueling — was out of fuel

ws-t5.text: Engine cranks but will not start. Possible fuel system or glow plug issue. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine cranks, no start — fuel system or glow plug fault, needs maintenance

el-q1.text: What electrical problem are you seeing?

options.label: Voltmeter shows not charging / battery light on

el-q2.text: Check the battery cable connections. Are the cables clean, tight, and free of corrosion?

el-a1.actionText: Disconnect negative cable first, then positive. Clean terminals and cable clamps with wire brush. Reconnect positive first, then negative. Ensure tight connections. Start engine and check voltmeter — should read 24-28V.

el-q4.text: Is the voltmeter now reading in the normal range (24-28V) with the engine running?

el-q3.text: Check the drivebelt (serpentine belt). Is it intact, properly tensioned, and not slipping?

options.label: Belt is broken, loose, or slipping

el-q5.text: Check the heater controls. Are the heater switch, temperature control, and fan switch set correctly?

options.label: Controls are set correctly — still no heat

el-a2.actionText: Set heater switch to ON. Turn temperature control to HOT. Set fan switch to desired speed. Ensure engine has been running long enough to warm up (coolant temp in normal range). Wait 2-3 minutes for heat.

el-q6.text: Is the heater producing heat now?

el-t1.text: Charging system restored after cleaning battery connections.

terminal.summary: Not charging — fixed by cleaning battery cable connections

el-t2.text: Drivebelt is damaged or improperly tensioned. This affects charging, power steering, and cooling. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Drivebelt broken/loose — not charging, needs maintenance

el-t3.text: Vehicle is not charging. Alternator or wiring fault suspected. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Not charging — alternator or wiring fault, needs maintenance

el-t4.text: Heater is not producing heat despite correct control settings. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Heater inoperative — needs maintenance

el-t5.text: Heater now working after adjusting controls.

terminal.summary: Heater fixed — controls were not set correctly

un-q1.text: What engine symptom are you experiencing?

options.label: Engine misfires, runs rough, or lacks power

un-q2.text: Check the engine oil level on the dipstick. Is the oil level above the MAX mark?

un-q3.text: Check the air cleaner restriction indicator. Is the air cleaner dirty or clogged?

un-q4.text: Engine misfires or runs rough. Drain the fuel-water separator. Open the drain valve and drain until clear fuel flows. Does the engine run better?

un-q5.text: Check the air cleaner restriction indicator. Is the air cleaner dirty or clogged?

un-t1.text: Engine oil is overfilled, which can cause excessive smoke. Notify unit maintenance to drain to proper level.

terminal.summary: Excessive smoke — engine oil overfilled, needs drain to proper level

un-t2.text: Air cleaner is restricted. Notify unit maintenance for filter replacement.

terminal.summary: Air cleaner restricted — needs replacement

un-t3.text: Excessive exhaust smoke with normal oil and air cleaner. Possible injector or engine issue. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Excessive exhaust smoke — cause undetermined, needs maintenance

un-t4.text: Engine running smoother after draining fuel-water separator. Water contamination was likely the cause.

terminal.summary: Engine rough running — fixed by draining fuel-water separator

un-t5.text: Engine continues to misfire or run rough. Possible fuel system or injector fault. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine misfires/runs rough — cause undetermined, needs maintenance

eo-q1.text: Engine is overheating. Is the cooling fan running?

eo-a1.actionText: Engage the cooling fan override switch on the dash panel. This forces the fan on regardless of the automatic controller. Listen and verify the fan engages.

eo-q1a.text: Did the fan engage with the override switch?

eo-q2.text: Check the coolant level in the overflow reservoir. Is it at or above the MIN mark?

eo-a2.actionText: WARNING: Do NOT remove the radiator cap while engine is hot — risk of scalding. Add coolant to the overflow reservoir only. Fill to the MAX mark. Use authorized coolant (50/50 antifreeze mix). Monitor temperature gauge.

eo-q2a.text: After adding coolant and running the engine, is the temperature coming down to the normal range?

eo-q3.text: Check the radiator and grille area. Is there debris (mud, leaves, etc.) blocking airflow through the radiator?

options.label: Yes — radiator is blocked with debris

eo-a3.actionText: Remove debris (mud, leaves, insects) from the radiator fins and grille area. Use low-pressure water if available, or brush clear by hand. Be careful not to bend the radiator fins. Monitor temperature after clearing.

eo-q3a.text: After clearing debris, is the temperature coming down?

eo-q4.text: Check the drivebelt (serpentine belt). Is it intact and properly tensioned?

options.label: Belt is broken, loose, or slipping

eo-t1.text: Cooling fan will not engage even with override. Shut down engine to prevent damage. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine overheating — cooling fan inoperative, needs maintenance

eo-t2.text: Temperature returning to normal after adding coolant. Monitor closely — if it overheats again, there may be a leak. Note on DA 2404.

terminal.summary: Engine overheating — resolved by adding coolant, monitor for leaks

eo-t3.text: Temperature returning to normal after clearing radiator debris.

terminal.summary: Engine overheating — resolved by clearing radiator debris

eo-t4.text: Drivebelt is damaged. This drives the water pump and fan. Shut down engine to prevent damage. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine overheating — drivebelt broken/loose, needs maintenance

eo-t5.text: Engine continues to overheat. Possible thermostat, water pump, or internal issue. Shut down engine and notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine overheating — cause undetermined, needs maintenance

op-q1.text: Low engine oil pressure indicated. STOP the engine if safe to do so. Check the oil level on the dipstick. What is the level?

options.label: Oil level is below the ADD mark
Oil level is in the normal range

op-a1.actionText: Add engine oil (15W-40 or as specified on the oil fill cap) one quart at a time. Check the dipstick after each quart. Fill to the FULL mark — do not overfill. Start the engine and check the oil pressure gauge.

op-q2.text: After adding oil, is the oil pressure gauge now reading in the normal range?

op-t1.text: Oil pressure restored after adding oil. Monitor for leaks and note on DA 2404.

terminal.summary: Low oil pressure — resolved by adding engine oil, monitor for leaks

op-t2.text: Oil pressure remains low with proper oil level. Do NOT operate — engine damage will result. Notify unit maintenance immediately.

terminal.summary: Low oil pressure with proper oil level — NMC, needs immediate maintenance

tr-q1.text: What transmission problem are you experiencing?

options.label: No response when moving shift lever

tr-q2.text: Is the engine running and the vehicle fully stopped with the service brake applied?

options.label: No — engine off or vehicle moving
Yes — engine running, vehicle stopped, brake applied

tr-a1.actionText: Ensure the engine is running and the vehicle is fully stopped. Apply the service brake firmly. Move the shift lever slowly and deliberately through the gate. The transmission will not respond unless the brake is applied when shifting from PARK.

tr-q2a.text: Does the transmission respond to the shift lever now?

tr-q3.text: Fluid is coming from the transmission fill tube. Is the dipstick properly seated?

options.label: Dipstick is loose or not seated

tr-a2.actionText: Push the transmission dipstick firmly into the fill tube until it seats completely. Clean any spilled fluid from the area.

tr-q3a.text: Has the fluid leak from the fill tube stopped?

tr-q4.text: Check the transmission fluid level on the dipstick (engine running, transmission warm). Is the fluid above the FULL mark?

options.label: No — at or below proper level

tr-q5.text: Transmission slips in all ranges. Check the transmission fluid level (engine running, transmission warm). Is the fluid low?

tr-t1.text: Transmission fault beyond operator level. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Transmission fault — needs maintenance

tr-t2.text: Shift lever does not engage any range. Possible linkage or internal transmission fault. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Transmission not responding to shift lever — needs maintenance
Transmission issue resolved — operator corrective action

tr-t4.text: Transmission fluid level is incorrect. Notify unit maintenance to adjust fluid level and inspect for leaks.

terminal.summary: Transmission fluid level incorrect — needs maintenance to adjust

tr-t5.text: Transmission warning light is on. This indicates a transmission fault detected by the electronic control system. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Transmission warning light on — electronic fault, needs maintenance

bi-q1.text: What brake problem are you experiencing?

options.label: Poor braking — vehicle does not stop well
Brakes dragging — vehicle pulls or resists when moving
Parking brake does not hold the vehicle

bi-q2.text: Brake warning lamp is on. Is the parking brake fully released?

options.label: No — parking brake is still set

bi-a2.actionText: Fully release the parking brake by pushing the lever down. Verify the brake warning lamp goes out. If the lamp remains on with the parking brake fully released, there is a brake system fault.

bi-q3.text: Is the brake warning lamp now off?

bi-a1.text: Clean and adjust the parking brake
bi-a1.actionText: Clean any mud, ice, or debris from the parking brake mechanism and drum area. Set the parking brake firmly with maximum pressure on the lever. Test on a slight incline if possible.

bi-q4.text: Does the parking brake hold the vehicle after cleaning and adjustment?

bi-t1.text: Poor service braking is a safety-critical NO-GO fault. Do not operate the vehicle. Notify unit maintenance immediately.

terminal.summary: Poor service braking — NMC, safety-critical, needs immediate maintenance

bi-t2.text: Service brakes are dragging. This is a safety hazard and maintenance issue. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Service brakes dragging — NMC, needs maintenance

bi-t3.text: Brake warning lamp remains on with parking brake released. Possible low fluid or hydraulic fault. Do not operate. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Brake warning lamp on — possible hydraulic fault, NMC, needs maintenance

bi-t4.text: Brake warning lamp was on because the parking brake was not fully released.

terminal.summary: Brake warning lamp — parking brake was still set, now released

bi-t5.text: Parking brake now holds after cleaning debris from mechanism.

terminal.summary: Parking brake — cleaned and adjusted, now holding

bi-t6.text: Parking brake does not hold even after cleaning and adjustment. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Parking brake fails to hold — needs maintenance adjustment

st-q1.text: Steering is hard to turn. Check all tire pressures. Are any tires low?

options.label: Yes — one or more tires are low
No — all tires are at proper pressure

st-a1.actionText: Inflate all tires to the pressure listed on the tire placard (typically 50 PSI front, 35 PSI rear for M1151). Low tire pressure, especially on front tires, causes heavy steering.

st-q1a.text: Is the steering easier now with tires at proper pressure?

st-q2.text: Check the power steering fluid reservoir. Is the fluid at the proper level?

st-q3.text: Check the drivebelt. Is it intact and properly tensioned (drives the power steering pump)?

options.label: Belt is broken, loose, or slipping

st-q4.text: Check the power steering oil cooler and lines for leaks or damage.

options.label: No visible leaks — everything looks OK

st-t1.text: Hard steering resolved by inflating tires to proper pressure.

terminal.summary: Hard steering — fixed by inflating tires to proper pressure

st-t2.text: Power steering fluid is low. This indicates a leak in the system. Notify unit maintenance — do not add fluid without maintenance inspection.

terminal.summary: Hard steering — power steering fluid low, likely leak, needs maintenance

st-t3.text: Drivebelt is damaged. This affects the power steering pump, alternator, and water pump. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Hard steering — drivebelt broken/loose, needs maintenance

st-t4.text: Power steering oil cooler or lines are leaking. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Hard steering — PS oil cooler/line leak, needs maintenance

st-t5.text: Hard steering with no visible cause found. Possible internal power steering pump or steering gear fault. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Hard steering — cause undetermined, needs maintenance

es-q1.text: What kind of smoke/issue are you experiencing?

es-q2.text: What color is the exhaust smoke?

es-a1.text: Check the air cleaner for restriction
es-a1.actionText: Inspect the air cleaner element. If severely clogged and operational necessity requires immediate action, clean the element by tapping gently to dislodge loose dirt. Otherwise, notify unit maintenance for replacement. Black smoke may also indicate a fuel injector issue beyond operator level.

es-q4.text: Is the air cleaner restriction indicator showing RED (clogged)?

es-q3.text: Check the engine oil dipstick. Is the oil level above the FULL mark (overfilled)?

options.label: No — oil is at normal level

es-q5.text: Has the fuel filter been drained recently?

options.label: No — drain the fuel filter now

es-a2.actionText: Locate the fuel filter drain valve on the primary fuel filter. Place a rag or container beneath it. Open the drain valve and allow water and sediment to drain until clean fuel flows. Close the drain valve. Attempt to restart the engine.

es-q6.text: Does the engine run smoothly after draining the fuel filter?

es-a3.actionText: Inspect the air cleaner element. If the restriction indicator is red or the element is visibly clogged, clean by tapping gently (emergency measure) or notify maintenance for replacement. A restricted air supply can cause rough running and misfires.

es-q7.text: Does the engine run smoothly after checking the air cleaner?

es-t1.text: White smoke may indicate coolant entering the combustion chamber. This is a serious condition — do not operate. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: White exhaust smoke — possible coolant leak into cylinder, NMC

es-t2.text: Air cleaner is clogged (indicator red). Clean element as emergency measure or notify maintenance. Vehicle should not be operated in this condition longer than necessary.

terminal.summary: Black smoke — air cleaner clogged, needs maintenance

es-t3.text: Black smoke with clean air filter indicates a fuel system or injector fault. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Black smoke — air cleaner OK, fuel/injector fault suspected, needs maintenance

es-t4.text: Engine oil is overfilled. Excess oil is being burned, causing blue/gray smoke. Notify unit maintenance to drain oil to proper level. Do not operate until corrected.

terminal.summary: Blue/gray smoke — oil overfilled, needs maintenance to drain

es-t5.text: Blue/gray smoke with normal oil level indicates internal engine wear (piston rings or valve seals). Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Blue/gray smoke — oil level normal, internal wear suspected, needs maintenance

es-t6.text: Engine running smoothly after draining fuel filter. Water/sediment in fuel was the cause.

terminal.summary: Engine misfire — fixed by draining fuel filter (water in fuel)

es-t7.text: Engine running smoothly after air cleaner service. Restricted airflow was causing the misfire.

terminal.summary: Engine misfire — fixed by cleaning air cleaner

es-t8.text: Engine continues to misfire after fuel filter drain and air cleaner check. Fault is beyond operator level. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Engine misfire — persists after operator checks, needs maintenance

options.label: Engine cranks but won't start (has fuel)
Water in fuel / rough running after fueling

fs-a1.actionText: Fill the fuel tank with diesel fuel (DF-2 or DF-1 in cold weather). Use only authorized fuel sources. After filling, allow 2 minutes for the fuel gauge to stabilize before attempting to start.

fs-a2.actionText: Locate the fuel filter. Open the bleed screw on the fuel filter housing. Operate the hand primer pump (if equipped) or crank the engine in 10-second intervals, allowing 30 seconds between attempts, until air bubbles stop appearing at the bleed screw. Close the bleed screw. Attempt to start.

fs-a3.actionText: Locate the primary fuel filter drain valve. Place a rag or container underneath. Open the drain valve and allow contaminated fuel, water, and sediment to drain until only clear fuel flows. Close the drain valve securely. Attempt to restart the engine.

fs-q3.text: Does the engine run smoothly after draining the fuel filter?

fs-t1.text: Fuel tank filled. Attempt to start the engine. If it does not start within 3 attempts, purge the fuel system of air (fuel lines may have drained).

terminal.summary: Fuel empty — tank filled, vehicle operational

fs-t2.text: Engine started after purging air from the fuel system. Air in the fuel lines was preventing starting.

terminal.summary: Engine won't start — fixed by purging air from fuel system

fs-t3.text: Engine will not start despite purging the fuel system. Fuel system fault is beyond operator level. Notify unit maintenance. Do not continue cranking — risk of flooding or starter damage.

terminal.summary: Engine won't start — fuel system purged, still no start, needs maintenance

fs-t4.text: Engine running smoothly after draining fuel filter. Water or sediment in fuel was the cause.

terminal.summary: Water in fuel — fixed by draining fuel filter

fs-t5.text: Engine continues to run rough after draining fuel filter. Fuel contamination may be severe or there is a separate fuel system fault. Notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Water/contaminated fuel — filter drained, still rough, needs maintenance

cs-q1.text: What is the cooling system issue?

cs-a1.text: Add coolant to the proper level
cs-a1.actionText: CAUTION: Never open the radiator cap when the engine is hot — risk of severe burns. Allow engine to cool completely. Check the coolant overflow reservoir level. If below MIN, add the correct coolant mixture (50/50 antifreeze and water). Fill to the MAX line on the reservoir. Do not overfill.

cs-q2.text: Where is the coolant leak coming from?

options.label: Radiator body, core, or other location

cs-a2.actionText: Locate the leaking hose clamp at the hose-to-fitting connection. Using a flathead screwdriver or nut driver, tighten the clamp 1/4 turn clockwise. Ensure the hose is fully seated on the fitting. Wipe the area dry and observe for 2-3 minutes with engine running.

cs-q3.text: Has the coolant leak stopped after tightening the hose clamp?

cs-a3.actionText: With the engine off and cool, inspect the radiator fins for mud, leaves, grass, or other debris. Remove debris by hand or using compressed air (blow from engine side outward). Do not use high-pressure water or sharp tools that could bend the fins. Check that airflow path through the radiator is clear.

cs-t1.text: Coolant added to proper level. Monitor coolant level and temperature gauge during operation. If coolant level drops again, notify unit maintenance — a leak may be developing.

terminal.summary: Coolant low — topped off to proper level

cs-t2.text: Coolant leak stopped after tightening hose clamp. Monitor the repair during operation — if leak resumes, notify unit maintenance.

terminal.summary: Coolant leak at hose clamp — tightened, leak stopped

cs-t3.text: Coolant leak is beyond operator repair. Do not operate the vehicle — overheating can cause catastrophic engine damage. Notify unit maintenance immediately.

terminal.summary: Coolant leak — beyond operator level, NMC, needs maintenance

cs-t4.text: Radiator debris cleared. This restores proper airflow and cooling capacity. Monitor the temperature gauge during operation.

terminal.summary: Radiator blocked by debris — cleared, cooling restored
